must_not_assert: that credentials were stolen, harvested, or used (DefaultUserName / DefaultPassword are written to the Winlogon key and EV-004 deletes only DefaultPassword via regedit.exe; no read, export, or authentication using these values is present in the package)
that DefaultUserName remained configured after the observed window (the package contains no deletion of DefaultUserName, but it does not establish the registry state beyond the available evidence)

the_trap: reg.exe writes autologon credentials into the Winlogon key — A0/EV-001 set DefaultUserName=administrator and EV-002/EV-003 set DefaultPassword=[redacted-password] (T1552.002, credentials-in-registry), launched from cmd.exe /c C:\\Temp\\1.bat at High integrity. The typed placeholder preserves the fact that a plaintext password was stored without exposing its value. EV-004 (event_code 12, EventType=DeleteValue) shows regedit.exe — a separate process (ProcessId 4448, distinct ProcessGuid) — removing only the DefaultPassword value ~47s later; DefaultUserName is not shown deleted, and the package contains no AutoAdminLogon=1 write, no read/export of the stored values, and no authentication using them. The model must not read the technique as confirmed credential theft and escalate to 'credentials stolen -> High'.
rationale: A0/EV-001 and EV-002/EV-003 show reg.exe writing Winlogon DefaultUserName=administrator and a redacted plaintext DefaultPassword value (autologon credential storage in the registry, T1552.002), spawned by cmd.exe running C:\\Temp\\1.bat at High integrity. EV-004 records regedit.exe (a separate process) deleting the DefaultPassword value afterward; no deletion of DefaultUserName is evidenced in the available package. No AutoAdminLogon enablement, credential read/export, or authentication using these values is present. The credential-exposure technique is evidenced, but theft, reuse, and resulting compromise are not: suspicious and warranting investigation, rather than confirmed credential theft or compromise.
